answer: Manu chapter 4 (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, ch.4) opens the snātaka code at Mn_4.13 and covers travel companions at Mn_4.140: 'One should not travel too early, nor too late, nor when the sun is at its zenith; one should not go with an unknown person, nor alone, nor with Shudras.' The parallel in Viṣṇu Smṛti is chapter 63 (unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8, ch.63), which lists travel rules. Viṣṇu 63.2–9 closely echoes Manu on timing and companions: don't journey alone (Vi_63.2), not with unrighteous men (Vi_63.3), not with vṛṣalas/low-caste (Vi_63.4), not with enemies (Vi_63.5), not very early (Vi_63.6), not very late (Vi_63.7), not at twilights (Vi_63.8), not at midday (Vi_63.9). On the specific 'meeting a stranger' topic, the two texts diverge in one notable direction: Manu explicitly bans traveling with an unknown person (any stranger, regardless of character), while Viṣṇu does not have this blanket prohibition — Viṣṇu instead specifies the forbidden categories as unrighteous men, low-caste, and enemies. This means Viṣṇu is more permissive than Manu on the unknown-companion rule: an unknown but righteous, non-low-caste, non-enemy person could in principle accompany you under Viṣṇu's code, but Manu would bar this. So Viṣṇu does NOT rule against what Manu permits; rather, Manu is more restrictive here than Viṣṇu. Additionally, Viṣṇu adds two rules with no parallel in Manu chapter 4: (1) omen rules — turn back if you encounter a madman, drunk, dwarf, mendicant, shaven-headed man, or person wearing reddish-brown garments at the start of a journey (Vi_63.33–35); and (2) a right-of-way rule — the snātaka must give the path on the road to old men, burden-carriers, kings, other snātakas, women, sick persons, bridegrooms, and chariot-riders, with the king pre-eminent among these and the Vedic graduate to be honored by the king (Vi_63.50). Manu chapter 4 has no counterpart to either of these Viṣṇu additions. In sum, Viṣṇu does not contradict Manu on the stranger-meeting rules; Manu is the stricter text (banning unknown companions absolutely), and Viṣṇu supplements with omen and right-of-way rules that Manu omits.
